questions.question: What is the strongest first principle when choosing a shard key?
options: Pick the field with the shortest name so the API stays simple.
Pick the field that best matches the dominant access pattern and locality boundary.
Pick a random UUID because it guarantees every query will be cheap.
Pick the oldest column in the table to avoid migration work.
questions.explanation: A shard key should be selected from workload shape, locality, and operational risk, not convenience.

questions.question: Why can sharding by random object ID be a poor default for a multi-tenant SaaS product?
options: It often destroys tenant-local queries and turns common reads into cross-shard fanout.
It prevents replication from working in distributed databases.
It makes every write strongly consistent across all regions automatically.
It removes the need for routing or metadata services.
questions.explanation: Random IDs help spread load, but they can badly misalign with tenant-scoped reads and writes.

questions.question: When is sharding by tenant ID usually strongest?
options: When most important queries, write ownership, and isolation requirements are tenant-scoped.
When the system only runs global analytical scans on every request.
When the dominant goal is to eliminate directory metadata entirely at any cost.
When all tenants have identical volume and no one ever needs to move data.
questions.explanation: Tenant sharding is strongest when the workload and ownership boundary are both tenant-local.

questions.question: What is the main trade-off of introducing hashed tenant buckets instead of raw tenant ID placement?
options: You gain smoother distribution, but routing and future tenant moves become more complex.
You guarantee every cross-shard query disappears forever.
You remove the need for per-tenant observability because balance is automatic.
You make hotspotting impossible even for a single very large customer.
questions.explanation: Bucketed hashing can improve balance, but it adds indirection and migration complexity.

questions.question: Which metric best exposes whether the shard key is mismatched to the real workload?
options: The percentage of critical-path requests that require scatter-gather across many shards.
The total number of markdown files in the repository.
The amount of free disk space on developer laptops.
The average length of API method names.
questions.explanation: Frequent scatter-gather on important flows is a clear sign the key does not match workload locality.

questions.question: What is the real danger of optimizing only for even distribution when picking a shard key?
options: You may create a nicely balanced system that performs badly on the queries users actually run.
You will be unable to use caching or replication anywhere in the architecture.
You automatically force all writes into one partition.
You make every migration impossible regardless of tooling.
questions.explanation: Distribution matters, but a balanced system with poor locality can still be slow and expensive.

questions.question: If one tenant starts producing 15% of all traffic, what is the best senior-level response?
options: Acknowledge that no shard key can ever change once deployed.
Consider tenant splits, dedicated placement, or bucketed routing while preserving tenant-local APIs.
Move every tenant to a random object ID scheme immediately with no migration plan.
Delete the tenant from hot dashboards so operators are not distracted.
questions.explanation: Large tenants often require explicit isolation or split strategies, not denial.

questions.question: Why might a directory service be worth adding to a sharded design?
options: It enables more flexible placement and controlled moves when self-locating keys are too rigid.
It guarantees zero-latency routing even during network partitions.
It removes every need for versioning during migration.
It makes analytical queries local to a single shard automatically.
questions.explanation: Directory indirection can make moves, placement control, and gradual migration far safer.
